perturbation.original_text: MA will at all times during the term of this Agreement maintain appropriate technical and organizational measures to protect any end-user data that it collects, accesses or processes in connection with this Agreement against unauthorized or unlawful use, disclosure, processing or alteration.
perturbation.changed_text: MA will, when practical, during the term of this Agreement consider implementing technical and organizational measures to protect end-user data that it may collect, access, or process in connection with this Agreement against potentially unauthorized or unlawful use, disclosure, processing, or alteration.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires MA to maintain 'appropriate' data protection measures. The modified text introduces ambiguity by stating 'when practical' and 'consider implementing,' which makes the obligation discretionary. Also, changing 'any' to 'end-user data that it may collect' limits the obligation and provides leeway, which conflicts with data protection laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts NRS 603A.210, which requires businesses in Nevada to implement and maintain reasonable security measures to protect personal information. The phrase 'when practical' and 'consider implementing' creates a loophole, allowing MA to potentially forego necessary security measures if they deem it impractical, which is a violation of the requirement for 'reasonable security measures'.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: NRS: CHAPTER 603A - SECURITY AND PRIVACY OF PERSONAL INFORMATION [Rev. 12/9/2024 4:09:20 PM--2024R1] CHAPTER 603A - SECURITY AND PRIVACY OF PERSONAL INFORMATION SECURITY OF INFORMATION MAINTAINED BY DATA COLLECTORS AND OTHER BUSINESSES General Provisions NRS 603A.010 Definitions. NRS 603A.020 Breach of the security of the system data defined. NRS 603A.030 Data collector defined. NRS 603A.040 Personal information defined. NRS 603A.100 Applicability; waiver of provisions prohibited. Regulation of Business Practices NRS 603A.200 Destruction of certain records. NRS 603A.210 Security measures. NRS 603A.215 Security measures for data collector that accepts payment card; use of encryption; liability for damages; applicability. NRS 603A.217 Alternative methods of and technologies for encryption: Adoption of regulations. NRS 603A.220 Disclosure of breach of security of system data; methods of disclosure; applicability. Remedies and Penalties NRS 603A.260 Violation constitutes deceptive trade practice. NRS 603A.270 Civil action. NRS 603A.280 Restitution. NRS 603A.290 Injunction. NOTICE REGARDING PRIVACY OF INFORMATION COLLECTED ON INTERNET FROM CONSUMERS General Provisions NRS 603A.300 Definitions. NRS 603A.310 Consumer defined. NRS 603A.320 Covered information defined. NRS 603A.323 Data broker defined. NRS 603A.325 Designated request address defined. NRS 603A.330 Operator defined. NRS 603A.333 Sale defined. NRS 603A.337 Verified request defined. NRS 603A.338 Applicability of provisions. Regulation of Business Practices NRS 603A.340 Notice regarding covered information collected by operator: Operator required to make available to consumers; contents; exception. NRS 603A.345 Submission of verified request to operator not to sell covered information collected by operator; response to verified request. NRS 603A.346 Submission of verified request to data broker not to sell covered information purchased by data broker; response to verified request. NRS 603A.347 Data broker authorized to remedy first failure to comply with requirements concerning verified request. NRS 603A.348 Operator authorized to remedy first failure to comply with notice requirements. NRS 603A.349 Operator authorized to remedy first failure to comply with requirements concerning verified request. Unlawful Acts, Penalties and Remedies NRS 603A.350 Unlawful acts. NRS 603A.360 Enforcement by Attorney General; civil penalty for violation or injunction; no private right of action against operator; provisions not exclusive. SECURITY AND PRIVACY OF CONSUMER HEALTH DATA
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity and discretion where the original text mandated action, potentially undermining data protection requirements. The law explanation points to NRS 603A.210 requiring 'reasonable security measures.' By changing 'will at all times maintain appropriate' to 'will, when practical, during the term of this Agreement consider implementing,' the modified text allows MA to potentially forego necessary security measures if they deem it impractical. This conflicts with the requirement for 'reasonable security measures' as indicated by the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 2 generally references Nevada law chapter 603A regarding security and privacy, and points to NRS 603A.210 which relates to security measures. The permissive language introduced significantly weakens the original obligation, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: MA shall protect copyrights, tradenames, trademarks, service marks, trade secrets and other confidential proprietary rights and information of Company and its affiliates and report promptly any infringements or suspected infringements of which MA becomes aware and to cooperate fully with Company in its efforts to protect its copyrights, tradenames, trademarks, service marks, trade secrets and other confidential proprietary rights and information.
perturbation.changed_text: MA should consider protecting copyrights, tradenames, trademarks, service marks, trade secrets and other confidential proprietary rights and information of Company and its affiliates and report infringements or suspected infringements of which MA becomes reasonably aware and cooperate as is convenient with Company in its efforts to protect its copyrights, tradenames, trademarks, service marks, trade secrets and other confidential proprietary rights and information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text makes it mandatory for MA to protect the company's intellectual property and report any infringements. The modified text weakens this obligation by using 'should consider', 'reasonably aware', and 'cooperate as is convenient'. This change makes the protection and reporting of infringements discretionary, instead of mandatory.
perturbation.law_explanation: By reducing the obligation to protect and report infringements of intellectual property to a mere consideration, it contradicts the spirit and intent of Nevada's intellectual property protection laws (NRS 600A.100), which aim to ensure adequate protections and remedies for intellectual property rights. The 'should consider' wording fails to provide the level of protection required, especially when it comes to reporting infringements, which are important for enforcement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: NRS: CHAPTER 600A - TRADE SECRETS (UNIFORM ACT) [Rev. 6/29/2024 4:37:37 PM--2023] CHAPTER 600A - TRADE SECRETS (UNIFORM ACT) NRS 600A.010 Short title. NRS 600A.030 Definitions. NRS 600A.032 Presumption of reasonable effort by owner to maintain secrecy; rebuttal of presumption. NRS 600A.035 Theft of trade secrets prohibited; criminal penalties. NRS 600A.040 Actual or threatened misappropriation: Injunctive relief. NRS 600A.050 Misappropriation: Damages. NRS 600A.055 Misappropriation and posting or dissemination on Internet: Effect. NRS 600A.060 Attorneys fees. NRS 600A.070 Preservation of secrecy. NRS 600A.080 Limitation of actions. NRS 600A.090 Effect of chapter on other law and remedies. NRS 600A.100 Inapplicability of chapter to acts which occurred before, or began before and continue after, July 1, 1987. _________ NRS 600A.010 Short title. This chapter may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added to NRS by 1987, 19 ) NRS 600A.030 Definitions. As used in this chapter, unless the context otherwise requires: 1. Improper means includes, without limitation: (a) Theft; (b) Bribery; (c) Misrepresentation; (d) Willful breach or willful inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy; (e) Willful breach or willful inducement of a breach of a duty imposed by common law, statute, contract, license, protective order or other court or administrative order; and (f) Espionage through electronic or other means. 2. Misappropriation means: (a) Acquisition of the trade secret of another by a person by improper means; (b) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (c) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (1) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; (2) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (I) Derived from or through a person who had used improper means to acquire it; (II) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (III) Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (3) Before a material change of his or her position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. 3. Owner means the person who holds legal or equitable title to a trade secret. 4. Person means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency,
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates the protection of intellectual property, while the modified text weakens this obligation to a discretionary "should consider." The original text mandates reporting infringements while the modified text changes this to reporting infringements MA is 'reasonably aware' of and to cooperate 'as is convenient'. According to the Law Explanation, this contradicts the spirit and intent of intellectual property protection laws which aim to ensure adequate protections and remedies for intellectual property rights. NRS 600A.030 defines misappropriation and includes the 'willful breach or willful inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy.' By making the protection and reporting discretionary, the modified contract undermines the duty to maintain secrecy, potentially leading to a breach, and contradicting the intention of NRS 600A.030.
